title: Provable Gradient Editing of Deep Neural Networks
abstract: In explainable AI, DNN gradients are used to interpret the prediction; in safety-critical control systems, gradients could encode safety constraints; in scientific-computing applications, gradients could encode physical invariants. While recent work on provable editing of DNNs has focused on input-output constraints, the problem of enforcing hard constraints on DNN gradients remains unaddressed. We present ProGrad, the first efficient approach for editing the parameters of a DNN to provably enforce hard constraints on the DNN gradients. Given a DNN $\mathcal{N}$ with parameters $\theta$, and a set $\mathcal{S}$ of pairs $(\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{Q})$ of input $\mathrm{x}$ and corresponding linear gradient constraints $\mathrm{Q}$, ProGrad finds new parameters $\theta'$ such that $\bigwedge_{(\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{Q}) \in \mathcal{S}} \frac{\partial}{\partial \mathrm{x}}\mathcal{N}(\mathrm{x}; \theta') \in \mathrm{Q}$ while minimizing the changes $\lVert\theta' - \theta\rVert$. The key contribution is a novel *conditional variable gradient* of DNNs, which relaxes the NP-hard provable gradient editing problem to a linear program (LP), enabling ProGrad to use an LP solver to efficiently and effectively enforce the gradient constraints. We experimentally evaluated ProGrad via enforcing (i) hard Grad-CAM constraints on ImageNet ResNet DNNs; (ii) hard Integrated Gradients constraints on Llama 3 and Qwen 3 LLMs; (iii) hard gradient constraints in training a DNN to approximate a target function as a proxy for safety constraints in control systems and physical invariants in scientific applications. The results highlight the unique capability of ProGrad in enforcing hard constraints on DNN gradients.

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
The paper introduces the problem of gradient editing wherein given a particular input to a network (here restricted to feed-forward networks with ReLU units), and a polyhedron within which the gradient  (or more accurately the Jacobian) of the network should lie, the approach computes changes to a subset of parameters, chosen so that the network is locally linear in these parameters. The approach builds upon similar ideas such as ARPNN that have appeared in the past (PLDI 2023), but extends to the problem of satisfying constraints
on the gradients rather than the outputs. The key idea is to formulate this as a linear program assuming that the change in the weights do not change the "activation patterns" of the various neurons in the network. The solution is applied to some very interesting case studies including enforcing "Grad-CAM" constraints on images wherein "Grad-CAM" refers to an approach to explainable classification wherein the gradient information is used to study some sort of sensitivity of the pixels to a classification. The paper uses the proposed gradient editing techniques to update weights of CNNs to make them robust to input perturbations. Other experimental evaluations include those on large language models and a place holder for a physical model with a gradient constraint.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
I enjoyed reading the paper for the most part. The writing and presentation were clear to me and the ideas were easy to see. I do have a few minor issues that I report at the end. 

+ The main strength of the paper lies in the applications. Although I am not an expert in the areas of computer vision or natural language processing, I found the applications to be well motivated and natural. I do not quite get the application to provably safe control systems: that application was illustrated by a rather artificial example that did not seem credible.

My key issues here are mostly about originality and some technical issues.

- Originality: I am not very sure about the  intellectual depth here. The idea that (i) fixing the activation patterns of the neuron linearizes a network is at the core of the paper and is hardly novel/original; (ii) the idea that one can modify a single layer's weights and the biases of the layers is also natural and has been explored by many authors even in the context of neural network repair. Nevertheless, a simple idea that can have practical impact is quite welcome.

- Technical applicability: I am not really sold that editing gradients at one input suffices. Clearly, it does not for most applications. However, the approach is limited to one layer at a time. I have been thinking about how one handles multiple inputs to the network whose gradients all need to lie within different polyhedra?  
   - It is not clear if editing a single layer at a time suffices. One imagines that multiple neurons across different layers may need to be modified. 
   - Editing more than one layer yields nonlinear (multilinear) constraints and approaches such as iterating between layers are well known not to be very good at solving these problems. 
   - Treating the inputs one by one is not going to work. Fixing for one input may invalidate a fix made for a previous input. 

Overall, interesting paper but I get the sense that the difficult problem has not been properly addressed here. Rather, a  simpler version is presented and it is unclear that ideas extend readily.

QUESTIONS:
- Please comment on the issue of how do you treat multiple inputs/multiple layers in your approach.

LIMITATIONS:
yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper introduces ProGrad , a method for editing the parameters of a deep neural network (DNN) to enforce hard constraints on its gradients. The key idea is to model DNN gradients using what the authors call a conditional variable gradient , which allows the originally non-linear and complex gradient constraint enforcement task to be reduced to solving a linear program (LP) . This makes it possible to efficiently find minimal parameter changes that ensure the DNN satisfies the desired gradient constraints.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
- Strengths:
  - This is an important and valuable research topic.
  - The paper is very well-written.
  - This work proposes an algorithm capable of provably enforcing hard gradient constraints on DNNs, not just soft regularization or empirical fine-tuning.
  - The conditional variable gradient formulation is mathematically well-grounded and allows for reducing the originally non-linear gradient editing problem into a linear programming one. This is a significant theoretical contribution.
  - The method is tested on diverse architectures and tasks — CNNs, LLMs, and regression functions, showing consistent success in satisfying gradient constraints where baselines fail.
- Weakness：
  - The current implementation only enforces gradient constraints at individual input points , not over neighborhoods or polytopes. While the paper mentions future extensions inspired by APRNN and PREPARED, this severely limits applicability in robustness and generalization settings. In practice, real-world safety constraints must hold across regions, not just isolated samples.
  - ProGrad’s "conditional variable gradient" (Def. 4.2) is an extension of APRNN’s conditional output. The chain rule (Eq. 16) directly applies APRNN’s framework to gradients, which seems to show little theoretical innovation beyond repurposing prior work.
  - The paper focuses on editing only the last few layers (typically the final layer) of the neural network. While this enables efficient and scalable editing of large models, it may limit the model’s ability to satisfy more complex or global gradient constraints that involve deeper layers.

QUESTIONS:
Does the method's restriction to editing only the final few layers affect its ability to enforce more complex or global gradient constraints? Could editing more layers improve constraint satisfaction, and what trade-offs might exist in terms of efficiency and performance?

LIMITATIONS:
- ProGrad edits only the last few layers (often just the final layer) of the neural network. This may limit the model’s ability to satisfy more complex or global gradient constraints that involve deeper layers.
- As the author said, ProGrad currently enforces gradient constraints only on a finite set of input samples. It does not provide guarantees for broader input domains or structured input regions.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
The paper proposes to edit the gradient of deep neural networks to satisfy hard polytope constraints by solving linear programming. The method is rooted in the formal verification of neural networks and is well-suited for constraint satisfaction. The experiments show some examples of potential applications on vision, LLM and NN training.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strengths:

- This paper is interesting. The topic of gradient satisfaction is important but challenging. The examples of the paper show potential applications in vision and LLMs. I believe it is also promising in formal methods and dynamical systems.

- The method is solid and the math notation is self-contained. The paper is clearly written and easy to follow. I enjoy reading the paper.

Weaknesses:

- One concern is that the LP decision variables only include the weight of first layer and biases of other layers, which may harm the feasibility given some bounded constraints of NN parameters. One suggestion would be to relax it to involve another weight parameter to make the constraint quadratic, which is also tractable for Gurobi, I think. It would be interesting to see the comparison between QP and LP.

- For the LLM example, I would like to know how scale the method can be. It is better to report the results and the required time for comparison of scalability. For NN training, it is interesting to see how this method can be applied to train Lipschitz NNs.

- Some related work can be discussed. Incomplete neural network verification can also involve gradient [1] for Lipschitz and verification of neural control barrier function also involves the gradient of neural networks [2], both of which can be casted as a special case of gradient constraint satisfaction. A recent DNN verification toolbox [3] can also be discussed.

- Minor: $\tilde \phi$ is not defined on Line 111 when it first appears.


--- 
[1] Shi et al. Efficiently computing local lipschitz constants of neural networks via bound propagation. NeurIPS 2022

[2] Hu et al. Verification of Neural Control Barrier Functions with Symbolic Derivative Bounds Propagation, CoRL 2024

[3] Wei et al. Modelverification. jl: a comprehensive toolbox for formally verifying deep neural networks, CAV 2025

QUESTIONS:
See weakness

LIMITATIONS:
Yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
ProGrad provides a helpful way to enforce constraints on the gradients of neural networks by editing their parameters. The experimental evaluations show how ProGrad can be used in interpretability (enforcing grad-cam constraints), editing weights of neural networks to fix hallucinations (llama 3), and in sys id and controls. The work shows significant improvement over just finetuning the models.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strengths

* Strong constraint enforcement is a great strength and the theory appears comprehensive.

* The ability to maintain these constraints is impactful in a few domains (interpretability, editing, sys id)

* The results show significant improvement in constraint satisfaction over just finetuning the models

Weaknesses

* I am uncertain if strictly enforcing gradients is helpful for some of the proposed applications. Example: does enforcing the gradient constraints on misclassified samples for LLama help reduce similar mistakes on held-out samples? Or is finetuning better in this case?  

Another example: in the system identification example in Section 5.3, what if the gradient of the function is not well behaved (such as it shooting to infinity at 0 or something else like that)? Will the system actually be approximated/identified accurately?

* Missing reference and discussion of a highly relevant work on enforcing hard constraints in [1]

[1] Sridhar et al, Guaranteed Conformance of Neurosymbolic (World) Models to Natural Constraints, L4DC 2023

QUESTIONS:
Please see weaknesses

LIMITATIONS:
yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This work proposes provable gradient editing using linear programming by relaxing nonlinear constant exact gradient of the deep neural networks (with ReLU activations) to linear variable gradients under constraint activation condition. While input/output constraint prior methods are available ProGrad is novel in applying constraints on gradients. Linear gradient expression and the activation condition is polynomial in the size and scales well.The authors present three applications: enforcing GradCAM constraints on ResNet152, Integrated Gradients (IG) on a Llama 3 LLM, and physical gradient constraints in scientific computation tasks. Their evaluation setting contains perturbed input which results in misclassification in the baseline  that is fixed by ProGrad by applying the constraints and solving with Linear Programming. Empirical evidence supports the author's claims about performance.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strengths:

1. The paper introduces the previously unaddressed problem of provable gradient editing i.e. editing DNN parameters to satisfy hard constraints on input gradients, rather than just output constraints. This is a significant and timely contribution.
2. Overall paper is clearly written with a well defined premise of definitions, theorems, proofs and overall mathematical rigor. Theorem 4.3 is the most fundamental one that is simple and elegant to follow and connects the relaxed problem to an LP, making what would otherwise be NP-hard tractable. This makes it to be solved by off the shelf LP solver in this case Gurobi.  
3. Performance achieved as measured by Constraint satisfaction, Cosine similarity, IOU, Classification Accuracy beats other fine tuning based methods. In all the cases it achieves 100% constraint satisfaction which is the main aim of the paper. So the main claim is well supported in results.
4. Wider domain coverage: Deep neural networks on image and language as well as control system proxy with simpler trigonometric function has been tested. 
5. Limitations are honestly acknowledged as the input is chosen to be constant unlike a polytope in most practical scenarios. Paper indicates some steps that can be taken from prior work to achieve this which is a good start.


Weaknesses:

1. Introduction lacks sufficient details about the motivation of adding constraints on gradients. It briefly explains grad cam attribution in fig.2, but lacks more details about other ways in which it can be used. For example it talks about requiring less data for training deep neural networks but does not sufficiently describe how or validate ProGrad indeed helps in this regard. 
2. Real-world LPs for large networks can still be computationally expensive. Therefore analysis of LP size and solve time as a function of network depth/width is required.
3. It only simulates the Control system application using a simpler trigonometric function. Effect on real scientific and safety critical control systems remains unexplored.
4. Lack of qualitative samples. Typically it is expected to provide more samples in supplementary but none other than a single sample in the main paper is provided. It would also help to showcase which samples are harder to be constrained and if there are any patterns.

QUESTIONS:
I have main question in the evaluation
1. LLM evaluation table (table 2) includes only generic term FT? Which method was tested here? Are all tested to average out the performance?
2. Does not sufficiently evaluate the effects on “correctly classified” inputs. And overall accuracy not just edit sets. Is it applied to all the samples?
3. Choice of the learned NN that is edited with ProGrad is not sufficiently explored. Please consider some ablations for readers to have more insights into the work.
4. Can authors include real datasets from control system applications? Can authors talk more about the effect on required dataset size for training?

LIMITATIONS:
yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
DECISION:
Accept (spotlight)

COMMENT:
The committee reached a consensus by which this is a strong manuscript that should be accepted. The work presents a novel and well-formulated theoretical perspective on gradient editing, with clear exposition and solid arguments. While reviewers initially raised some concerns, the authors provided thoughtful clarifications and additional points during the rebuttal that addressed these points satisfactorily. Overall, the combination of novelty, clarity, and potential applicability makes this a valuable contribution to the NeurIPS community. I recommend acceptance.
reviews.TITLE: Decision
